cards.title: Code Review as Knowledge Transfer
cards.teaser: The reviewer learns the codebase, the author gets a second perspective on design, and the team builds shared understanding — defect detection is a side effect, not the point.

runs.text: Most teams treat code review as a quality gate: a human check that runs before merging. That framing undersells it. The more durable value is what happens to the people involved.
When a reviewer reads your code carefully:

items.text: They understand a part of the codebase they didn't before — reducing the bus factor for that system.
They often catch design issues that automated tools miss: unclear boundaries, tight coupling, an abstraction that doesn't quite fit.
They build context that makes them faster on future reviews of the same area.

runs.text: When you receive a review:

items.text: You get a second set of assumptions applied to code you wrote while inside your own mental model.
Reviewers regularly surface the case you didn't consider, the name that isn't obvious to someone who didn't write the function, the dependency you'd implicitly relied on.

runs.text: The failure mode is treating review as a checkbox: a quick scan for obvious errors, an approval rubber-stamped to keep the queue moving. This produces the cost of the process (author waits, reviewer's time is spent) without the benefit. Teams that review this way build a false sense of quality assurance and no shared understanding.
A useful proxy: if the reviewer can't describe what the change does and why it exists after completing the review, the review wasn't thorough enough to transfer knowledge.
Review is a learning event for the reviewer, not just a validation event for the author. Treat it that way and you compound knowledge across the team instead of concentrating it.
It's like pair programming in retrospect — one person already took a path through the problem, and the other is being asked to understand it well enough to catch where it goes wrong.

cards.title: PR Descriptions That Help Reviewers
cards.teaser: A PR description that explains the why, the what to focus on, and the known tradeoffs makes a reviewer's job three times faster than one that says "fixes bug."

runs.text: Reviewers come to your PR cold. They don't have your mental model of the problem, the three approaches you considered and discarded, or the constraint that made you choose this implementation. Your description is the only tool you have to give them that context before they read a single line of code.
A PR description that works covers four things:
Why this change exists.
What user need, bug, or design decision caused this PR to be written? Without this, reviewers guess at intent and review against the wrong mental model.
What to focus on.
Not every line deserves equal scrutiny. Call out the parts where you made a real decision — "I changed the retry logic in
fetchUser
, wanted a second opinion on the backoff interval" — so reviewers can spend their time where it matters.
What was intentionally left out.
If you scoped the PR deliberately, say so. "Out of scope: error handling for the offline case — tracked in #482." This prevents reviewers from flagging known gaps as blocking issues.
Known tradeoffs.

If you made a pragmatic choice — "this adds an extra DB call in the hot path; acceptable for now because traffic is low" — say it. A reviewer who discovers this without context will flag it as a bug; a reviewer who knows you're aware of it can evaluate whether they agree with the tradeoff.
The description that says "fixes bug" forces the reviewer to reconstruct all of this from the diff, which takes longer and produces a shallower review.
Write the PR description before you open the PR — not as an afterthought. If you can't explain the why in two sentences, the design may not be settled enough to review.
A PR description is the diff's README — without it, the code tells you what changed, but not why it needed to.

cards.title: The Reviewer's Mindset
cards.teaser: Review for correctness first, maintainability second, and design third — and know the difference between "I would have done it differently" and "this will cause a problem."

runs.text: Review with a priority order, not a random scan. Correctness — does this code do what it's supposed to do without introducing bugs — is non-negotiable. Maintainability — will the next engineer be able to understand and change this — is important but usually not blocking. Design preferences — how you would have structured it — are often not worth raising at all.

The most common review failure is treating design preferences as blocking concerns. "I would have used a strategy pattern here" is not a blocker unless the current approach creates a specific, articulable problem. If you're going to raise it, be specific about the consequence: "this will make it hard to add new payment methods without modifying this class each time" is a reviewable concern; "I prefer the strategy pattern" is a personal preference.

A practical filter: before posting a comment, ask yourself whether you can name a specific problem the current code will cause — a bug, a maintenance burden with concrete examples, a performance issue at projected scale. If you can't, it's a suggestion, not a concern.
Where the linter has a rule, don't raise a style comment. Automate what can be automated and spend your review attention on what can't.

One more calibration: read the whole diff before leaving any comments. A question you have on line 12 is often answered on line 80. Commenting without full context generates noise that the author has to triage.
If half your review comments are style preferences that a formatter could have caught, you're spending your review attention on the wrong things — and training authors to discount your comments.
A good reviewer is like a structural engineer reviewing a blueprint — they're not there to redesign the building, they're there to find the places where it won't hold.

cards.title: Giving Feedback Constructively
cards.teaser: Distinguish blocking concerns from suggestions, use questions instead of directives, and separate the code from the person — or your comments will be ignored, argued with, or both.

runs.text: How you phrase feedback determines whether it gets acted on or defended against. The goal is to make it easy for the author to engage with the substance rather than the tone.
Distinguish blocking concerns from suggestions. "This will fail when
userId

is null" is blocking — it needs to be addressed before merge. "I'd consider using a constant here instead of a magic number" is a suggestion — the author can take it or leave it. If you don't mark the difference, authors have to guess, and they often guess wrong, either ignoring a real problem or spending time on a nit you didn't mean to insist on.

Use questions instead of directives where you're not certain. "What happens if this request times out?" opens a conversation. "Add timeout handling" closes it in a way that can feel adversarial. Questions also acknowledge that you might be missing context — the author may have handled the timeout three layers up, and a question surfaces that without embarrassment.

Separate the code from the person. "This function is doing too many things" is about the code. "You made this too complicated" is about the person. The first invites a fix; the second invites defensiveness.

Keep the ratio of concerns to affirmations honest. A review with 15 issues and no acknowledgment of what worked creates the impression that the author did everything wrong, which is rarely true. One sentence — "the overall structure is clean, a few things to address" — resets the framing.
Prefix non-blocking comments explicitly: "nit:", "suggestion:", "optional:" so the author immediately knows the priority without having to read tea leaves.
Good review feedback is like a compiler error message — the useful ones tell you exactly what's wrong and where; the unhelpful ones just say "failed."

cards.title: Receiving Feedback Without Defensiveness
cards.teaser: The review is on the code, not on you — and the response that closes the loop professionally is the one that earns you a reputation for being easy to work with.

runs.text: Review feedback that stings usually isn't personal, but it can feel that way when you've spent three days on a change and the first response is a list of problems. The cognitive trick that helps most: the reviewer is spending their time trying to improve the outcome. That's the action of someone invested in the work, not hostile to it.

The productive response to a review comment is one of three things: fix it, explain why you disagree, or ask a clarifying question. The unproductive response is to argue without engaging with the substance, or to silently not fix something and leave the comment unresolved.
When you disagree with a comment, say so directly and explain why. "I see it differently — this check handles that case at the call site, in
AuthMiddleware.swift
line 42" is a professional disagreement that gives the reviewer what they need to update their position. "This is fine" is not.

Close every comment when you push a revision. Either address it, explain why you didn't, or explicitly defer it with a ticket number. Leaving comments open after revision forces the reviewer to re-read the thread to determine what changed, which wastes their time and slows the merge.
When a reviewer raises something you genuinely missed, a simple "good catch" or "fixed" is enough. You don't need to explain why you missed it — that creates noise. The diff shows what changed.
The engineer who is easy to review builds a reputation for receiving feedback well, which means reviewers spend more time on their PRs, which means their code quality compounds faster.
Code review is a feedback loop, not a verdict — your job is to process the signal, not defend against it.

cards.title: Shared Code Ownership
cards.teaser: "This is my code" is an antipattern — single-author systems are single-failure-point systems, and rotating reviewers is one of the cheapest ways to reduce that risk.

runs.text: Collective code ownership means any engineer on the team can understand, modify, and debug any part of the codebase with reasonable effort. The opposite — where each module has a single person who really knows it — is a fragility that compounds over time.

The costs of concentrated ownership are concrete. When the expert is unavailable during an incident, the team loses hours reconstructing context they never had. When the expert leaves, the knowledge leaves too — and the codebase develops silent landmines that no one knows are there. New engineers avoid the "owned" modules because they feel like trespassing, which reinforces the pattern.

Review is one of the primary mechanisms for distributing ownership. When engineers regularly review code outside their immediate area, they build enough familiarity to move in that area when needed. The team that routes every review back to the original author is actively preventing this.
Concrete practices that build shared ownership:

items.text: Rotate reviewers deliberately — assign reviews across the team, not just to the person who last touched the file.
Pair on new modules before they solidify — the second person who understands the design becomes the backup owner.
Document the load-bearing constraints — what invariants does this system rely on, and where are they documented?

runs.text: The bus factor — how many engineers would have to leave before a system becomes unmaintainable — is a measurable proxy for ownership distribution. Teams that track it tend to improve it.

If there's a file in your codebase where a PR from anyone other than the original author makes the team nervous, that's a bus factor of one. That's not a personality trait to work around — it's a structural risk to address.
Concentrated code ownership is like a database with a single replica — fine until it isn't, and by the time it becomes a problem, you're already in the incident.

cards.title: Async vs Synchronous Code Review
cards.teaser: Async review is the default — the reviewer schedules it, the author isn't blocked waiting — but knowing when to escalate to a sync conversation is the skill that prevents days of slow back-and-forth.

runs.text: Async review works because it doesn't require the author and reviewer to be available simultaneously, which respects both parties' ability to schedule focused work. The author merges when approved; the reviewer reviews when it fits into their day. This is the correct default for the large majority of PRs.

The failure mode of async review is the long comment thread. When a back-and-forth reaches three or more exchanges without convergence, the marginal cost of another async round is high — each turn takes half a day — and the accumulated confusion is often worse than if they'd talked for ten minutes. If you're on round three of a comment thread about an architectural question, the right move is a 15-minute sync to resolve it and then document the outcome in the thread.
When to go synchronous:

items.text: A blocking disagreement that involves design-level ambiguity, not just implementation choices.
A junior engineer whose PR is in rough shape — a sync can give them 30 minutes of directed context that five rounds of async comments couldn't.
A change so large or architectural that written review is genuinely insufficient for the reviewer to build a mental model.

runs.text: Review SLA is a team contract, not an individual judgment. A team that agrees on "first response within one business day" sets a predictable cadence that authors can plan against. The absence of any SLA produces random latency — some PRs merge in an hour, some sit for a week — which makes it impossible to plan work or estimate delivery.
Set a team review SLA and treat violations as process failures, not personal failures. "We expect first review within one business day" is specific enough to hold and measure.
Async review is like message passing — efficient when the protocol is clear, slow and deadlock-prone when messages keep bouncing back without resolution.

cards.title: The Definition of Done for a PR
cards.teaser: Done" means tests pass, CI is green, coverage is maintained, docs are updated if needed, and at least one approval — not "the author thinks it's ready.

runs.text: The default definition of done — "I think it's good, merging" — produces inconsistency. One engineer merges with tests skipped because the deadline was close; another waits three days for a second approval when one would have been enough. Without an explicit contract, "done" is whatever the author decides, which varies by engineer and day.
A working definition covers each layer:
Tests pass.
Not just CI green — all tests, including locally, before pushing. A CI that's green because the failing tests were disabled is not green.
Coverage maintained.
New code has tests. "I'll add tests in a follow-up" is a commitment that evaporates in 80% of cases. If the code isn't testable without significant refactoring, that's a signal about the design.
Linter and formatter clean.
No suppressions without a comment explaining why. If the linter is wrong, update the linter config — don't suppress the rule.
Docs updated if the public API changed.

A new parameter, a changed return type, a removed endpoint — these need to be reflected somewhere a caller can find them. The PR that ships the change without updating the docs creates a lie that compounds.
At least one approval from someone who actually read the diff.
Rubber-stamp approvals don't count. If the team can't distinguish a thoughtful approval from a reflexive one, the approval requirement has no value.
CI green at the head commit.
Not "CI was green when I last checked" — green at the commit being merged.
Merging a PR that doesn't meet the team's definition of done is a social contract violation, not just a technical shortcut. It erodes the norms that make the bar meaningful for everyone else.
Done is a contract, not a feeling — the same way a function contract specifies preconditions and postconditions, a PR contract specifies what must be true before the change ships.

cards.title: Pair Programming — Not Slower, Just Different
cards.teaser: Pair programming replaces async review round-trips with an immediate feedback loop — and the cases where it adds most value are specific and worth recognizing.

runs.text: The criticism that pair programming is slow is understandable: two engineers working on one task looks like 50% productivity. The accounting is wrong because it ignores what's replaced. A solo engineer who spends three hours on a problem, opens a PR, waits a day for review, goes back and forth over two rounds of comments, and then rewrites half the approach has spent more total time — and more total engineering-hours — than two engineers who paired and shipped a cleaner solution the same afternoon.
Where pairing adds the most value:
Unfamiliar codebase.
One engineer navigates the domain; the other drives the implementation. Both leave with the understanding, not just the code.
Complex algorithm or system design.
Two people working through edge cases in real time catch more problems before they're written than one person writing and one person reviewing asynchronously.
New team member onboarding.

Pairing a senior engineer with a new hire on a real task is faster and more effective than documentation-only onboarding. The new hire ships something real; the senior engineer transfers context they'd otherwise write down inefficiently.
Debugging a hard problem.
A second set of eyes on a gnarly issue often resolves it faster than one person working alone for hours.

Where pairing is overkill: routine changes, well-understood work, tasks where the engineer is confident and the scope is narrow. Pairing on every change is a drag; pairing strategically is a multiplier.
The best pair programming sessions end with both engineers saying they couldn't have gotten there as fast alone. That's the signal you're using it in the right place.
Pairing is like a real-time compiler — it catches errors as they're written instead of waiting for the full compile to fail.

cards.title: Team Norms and Coding Standards
cards.teaser: Standards that can be automated should be — a linter running in CI ends the tabs-vs-spaces debate permanently, freeing code review for the decisions that actually require human judgment.

runs.text: There are two categories of standards, and confusing them wastes enormous amounts of team energy.
Standards that can be automated

are formatting rules, import ordering, naming conventions with consistent application, and any check that can be expressed as a linter rule. These should be enforced by a tool in CI, not by a human in a review. The team that still argues about trailing commas in 2024 is the team that hasn't run
gofmt
prettier
, or
swiftformat
on a pre-commit hook. Once automated, the standard is free — it requires no review attention, no negotiation, and no inconsistency across authors.
Standards that require judgment

are architectural choices (when to introduce a new service boundary), naming patterns that encode domain understanding (when to call something a "manager" vs a "coordinator" vs a "service"), and conventions that aren't mechanical enough to automate. These belong in a written team document — not in the reviewer's head.

The failure modes are symmetric. Teams that automate nothing spend review time on formatting debates that should have been resolved once. Teams that document nothing accumulate undocumented conventions that only long-tenured engineers know, and then enforce them inconsistently through review, which feels arbitrary to engineers who weren't there when the convention formed.

Document the conventions that require judgment. Run a formatter over everything else. Then spend review time on logic, design, correctness, and the things that actually differ between a good implementation and a bad one.

Before raising a code style comment in review, ask: can this be caught by a linter? If yes, add the rule. If no, check whether the convention is documented anywhere. If neither, you're enforcing an undocumented personal preference.
Automated standards are like compiler rules — you don't argue with the compiler about whether semicolons are necessary, you just write code that compiles and move on.
